items.title: $AMBA — Edge AI vision chip company with $800M Hanwha deal, trading at 87x NTM PE
items.idea: Ambarella has pivoted from GoPro/dashcam chips to a pure-play Edge AI semiconductor company. Their CVflow® architecture excels at processing high-resolution video and AI models with minimal power draw — critical for battery-powered and automotive applications. Edge AI now makes up ~80% of total revenue. Key moat: recently signed an $800M+ multi-year co-development agreement with Hanwha across security and robotics. Trades at 87x NTM PE and 51x NTM EV/FCF. Market cap $3.16B, net cash positive. CEO Fermi Wang has been at the helm since 2004.
items.angle: If the physical AI thesis (robots, ADAS, smart cameras everywhere) is correct, AMBA is the picks-and-shovels play at the edge. NVDA wins the data center; AMBA wins power-constrained inference. Different risk/reward but the same underlying secular theme.
items.tweetA: Everyone knows NVDA wins the data center. AMBA wins the edge. Power-efficient AI vision chips for cars, robots, security cameras. $800M Hanwha deal locked in. 80% of revenue now pure Edge AI. Nobody's watching this.
items.tweetB: $AMBA at 87x NTM PE sounds expensive until you model what it means if every car, camera, and robot needs a computer vision brain. The real question: can CVflow + high switching costs hold against Qualcomm and Mobileye at scale?

items.title: Goldman: markets can absorb the mega-IPO wave — but the real test is the 2027 lockup expiry cliff
items.idea: Goldman argues buybacks, M&A, household flows, foreign capital, and passive index demand provide meaningful support for the coming IPO pipeline. But the real test comes AFTER the IPOs: lockup expirations and insider selling could release hundreds of billions into the market during 2027 and beyond. Market resilience increasingly depends on a narrow AI leadership group. Note: Goldman is 'hardly a disinterested observer' as underwriter to these offerings.
items.angle: The float at IPO is always small and manageable. The $500B+ insider overhang hitting in 2027 when lockups expire is a different risk entirely.
items.tweetA: Goldman says markets can absorb the mega-IPO wave. I agree — the float is always small at launch. The question is 2027 when lockups expire and insiders start selling hundreds of billions into the same narrow AI-dependent market. Goldman is answering the easy question.
items.tweetB: The SpaceX IPO isn't the liquidity test. The lockup cliff is. Everyone rushing to IPO in 2026 = everyone selling in 2027. Goldman is technically right about supply absorption — until the insider overhang turns into a secondary avalanche.

items.title: Citi Economic Surprise Index just hit its highest reading since October 2023
items.idea: The Citi Economic Surprise Index has improved to the highest reading since Oct'23, meaning economic data is consistently coming in above economists' consensus forecasts. This stands in direct tension with rising weekly jobless claims and AI-driven layoffs simultaneously affecting the labor market.
items.angle: Classic late-cycle divergence — hard data beats while the labor market frays. Consensus was wrong on the downside in 2024-25; it may be wrong again now.
items.tweetA: Citi Economic Surprise Index just hit its highest reading since October 2023. Data is beating expectations across the board. But weekly jobless claims are rising and 40% of corporate layoffs are now AI-driven. Two economies running simultaneously. For now, the hard data wins.
items.tweetB: If the consensus was wrong about recession in 2024-25, it might be wrong about slowdown in 2026. Citi ESI at 2.5-year highs is hard to argue with. Don't fight the tape, and don't fight the data.

items.title: US air travel at 2.58M daily through June 1 — in line with past two years, well above 2022 low
items.idea: TSA checkpoint data through June 1 shows air travel at 2.58M daily, in line with the past two years and well above the 2022 post-pandemic low of 2.19M. Consumer travel demand remains robust despite macro uncertainty and tariff overhang.
items.angle: Real-time travel data is one of the cleanest unmanipulated consumer health indicators available. Until this breaks, skepticism of the doom narrative is warranted.
items.tweetA: 2.58M Americans screened at airports through June 1. In line with the last two years. Well above the post-pandemic low. Everyone waiting for the consumer to crack — they haven't. Discretionary demand for travel is the cleanest real-time consumer signal available.
items.tweetB: If there's a recession coming, nobody told the airports. TSA data through June 1: 2.58M daily — exactly in line with the prior two-year trend. The consumer thesis that keeps getting called wrong keeps being wrong.

items.title: Central banks resumed net gold purchases in April — 17 tonnes after March net sales
items.idea: Central banks bought a net 17 tonnes of gold in April after reporting sizeable net sales in March, per World Gold Council. The structural CB buying trend remains intact. Two separate bullish signals — structural CB demand and Tavi Costa's 40-year technical inflection — converging simultaneously.
items.angle: CB gold demand is the most durable bid in the market. It does not respond to Fed rate rhetoric or real yield changes. The March net-sale blip has reversed. The de-dollarization-driven CB accumulation continues.
items.tweetA: Central banks: net sellers in March, net buyers of 17 tonnes in April. The structural CB gold bid is intact. This is the demand that doesn't care about Fed policy, real yields, or dollar strength. It's the baseline the gold bulls are building on.
items.tweetB: 17 tonnes of central bank gold buying in April. Most gold coverage focuses on ETF flows and futures positioning — the CB bid is the structural anchor nobody covers adequately. And it just reconfirmed its trend after a one-month pause.

items.title: TS Lombard: AI demand bullish across all indicators — but 'overall AI adoption remains low'
items.idea: Per TS Lombard via Daily Chartbook: token expenditure is robust, memory chip shortage continues boosting prices, Nvidia datacenter revenue shows no sign of slowing. But the critical qualifier: 'overall AI adoption remains low.' The demand spike is concentrated among hyperscalers and early adopters. Mass enterprise and consumer deployment hasn't arrived yet — meaning the demand surge is happening BEFORE the main wave.
items.angle: If adoption is still low while demand is already this strong, the supply/demand imbalance gets worse when mainstream rollout actually happens. The capex cycle isn't over — it's in inning 3.
items.tweetA: TS Lombard on AI demand: 'Remain bullish across the board. Token expenditure robust. Memory shortage driving prices. Nvidia DC no sign of slowing.' The punchline: 'overall AI adoption remains low.' This isn't peak AI. It's pre-mainstream AI.
items.tweetB: Memory chips in shortage. Nvidia selling out. Token spend accelerating. And AI adoption is still LOW. If this is what early innings look like, what happens when 500M enterprise workers actually start using this stuff at scale?

charts.desc: AI demand dashboard — token spend, memory pricing, Nvidia DC revenue, adoption curve

items.title: Piper Sandler: CAPE ratio comparisons to history are structurally invalid — index composition changed everything
items.idea: Michael Kantro (Piper Sandler) argues that structural changes in the equity market have 'diminished the utility of comparing current valuations to historical norms.' An index heavy in growth/defensive sectors carries a higher structural multiple than a cyclical-heavy index. Today's S&P 500 — 35%+ mega-cap tech — has fundamentally different earnings quality than the 1970s or 2000s index.
items.angle: The permabear CAPE argument has been wrong for 13 years. Kantro gives it the structural burial it deserves. This doesn't mean markets can't fall — it means CAPE isn't a timing tool.
items.tweetA: Piper Sandler's Kantro: CAPE comparisons to historical norms are structurally broken. An index 35% mega-cap tech has different earnings duration than a 1970s cyclical-heavy index. You can't run a CAPE comparison across those two instruments and call it analysis.
items.tweetB: The valuation bears have been calling the top with CAPE since 2013. Kantro at Piper Sandler finally articulates WHY it's the wrong tool: composition change explains most of the premium. It's not exuberance — it's a fundamentally different index.

charts.desc: CAPE ratio vs sector composition decomposition

items.title: Democrats blocked the Lebanon war powers vote — Iran WPR passed 215-208, Lebanon WPR failed
items.idea: Iran war powers resolution passed 215-208 with 4 Republicans. Lebanon WPR was blocked when Democratic leaders moved against Tlaib's resolution. Key diagnostic: this is NOT Democrats rejecting war powers as a concept — they are choosing which battlefield. The Lebanon ceasefire has a 'missing actuator' — Hezbollah compliance cannot be commanded by a state-to-state agreement. Next statutory trigger: alternative Lebanon WPR must clear committee by ~June 18 under 50 U.S.C. §1546.
items.angle: Congress will constrain Iran operations but not Israel's Lebanon campaign. Hezbollah remains the uncontrolled variable in any Middle East diplomatic equation.
items.tweetA: Congress passed the Iran war powers resolution 215-208. Then blocked the Lebanon one. The tell: Democrats are not opposing war powers as a tool. They are opposing constraining Israel's Hezbollah campaign. The ceasefire has no mechanism to command a militia. June 18 is the next trigger.
items.tweetB: The Lebanon WPR failed because it didn't name Hezbollah. A trilateral US-Israel-Lebanon framework cannot command Hezbollah's compliance. Until that actuator exists, any Middle East ceasefire architecture is structurally incomplete.

items.source: Dark Side of the Boom / BNP Paribas
items.title: BNP Paribas: SpaceX's real story is the $75B free float — the largest liquidity rotation event in Wall Street history
items.idea: SpaceX targeting $86B raise at $1.8T valuation. BNP Paribas argues the real metric is the $75B free float. Passive fund index inclusion, retail FOMO, and leveraged ETF flows could all trigger simultaneously. Funding SpaceX purchases = selling something else, creating cross-asset volatility well beyond a single stock listing.
items.angle: BNP is identifying the second-order liquidity effect correctly. Watch index rebalancing flows in the weeks after listing for the real market signal.
items.tweetA: SpaceX IPO headline: $86B raise. The BNP number you need: $75B free float. That's $75B of capital that needs to come from somewhere. Passive funds, retail, leveraged ETFs all buying simultaneously. The volatility won't be in SpaceX — it'll be in whatever gets sold to fund it.
items.tweetB: BNP Paribas: SpaceX isn't just an IPO, it's a capital reallocation event. $1.8T valuation with mandatory index inclusion means passive money has no choice. The test isn't whether the IPO prices well — it's what the secondary market looks like 90 days later.

items.title: Nvidia CDS trade at lower spreads than US Treasuries — markets repricing sovereign vs. corporate risk
items.idea: Nvidia CDS now trade at a lower credit premium than US Treasuries. The market is pricing NVDA as a better credit risk than the US government — reflecting both deteriorating perceptions of US sovereign credibility AND the extraordinary cash position of AI mega-caps.
items.angle: When a single corporation is perceived as a safer credit than the world's reserve currency issuer, the implications for real rates, dollar reserve status, and sovereign risk premium are profound.
items.tweetA: Nvidia CDS now trade at LOWER spreads than US Treasuries. Read that again. The market is pricing NVDA as a better credit than the US government. This isn't a valuation anomaly — it's a statement about sovereign credibility vs. AI earnings power.
items.tweetB: Dauber this morning: 'Nvidia CDS trade at lower premium than these United States of America.' If you think US sovereign risk premium is just about deficits and bond auctions, this data point should expand your frame. The comparison set has changed.

items.title: Dauber: SNAFU normalization of political risk — Middle East chaos produced rotation, not abandonment
items.idea: Dauber catalogs SNAFU events (insider trading of geopolitical decisions, unqualified appointments including William Pulte from meme stocks to Director of National Intelligence). Key market observation: Middle East chaos produced rotation into financials — NOT capital abandonment. Capital stayed in the market.
items.angle: 'Rotation, not abandonment' is the two-word summary of the current regime. Every political shock redistributes capital; it does not destroy it.
items.tweetA: The two most important words in US equity markets right now: 'rotation, not abandonment.' Every political shock, every geopolitical headline — money rotates from one sector to another. Nobody leaves. That is the entire bull case in its simplest form.
items.tweetB: Dauber: William Pulte went from meme stocks (GameStop) to Director of National Intelligence, overseeing all US intelligence apparatus. The SNAFU normalization dynamic is real. Markets don't price it until there's a financial transmission mechanism.

items.title: Options price 4 things simultaneously: direction, volatility, time, and PATH — most traders only think about 1
items.idea: Options price direction, implied vol, time decay, and path dependency simultaneously. Most retail traders focus exclusively on direction. 'The market charges you for all four, every single time.' Cheap options are cheap because the market has already priced the path correctly. The gap between 'what you think you're buying' and 'what you're actually buying' is where most losses live.
items.angle: Framework for understanding why crowded retail call-buying drives gamma squeezes AND is a systematic wealth transfer from retail to market makers.
items.tweetA: Most options traders lose money because they think they're buying leveraged directional exposure. The market is charging you for direction + vol + time + PATH simultaneously. You thought about the first one. The house already priced all four. That's the gap.
items.tweetB: Lord Fed: 'Cheap options are usually cheap for a reason.' Most important sentence in options education. When you think you found a cheap call, ask what the market knows about the path that you don't.

items.title: Tavi Costa: Gold miners sitting on 40-year resistance turned major support — 'act accordingly'
items.idea: Tavi presents a sequence of charts arguing gold is in the early stages of a significant bottom. 'There is nothing more technically bullish than a 40-year resistance level turning into major support. That is exactly where gold miners sit today.' Full chart sequence behind paywall. Combine with CB buying resumption (Item 5) for two simultaneous bullish signals.
items.angle: A 40-year resistance flip is a generational signal if confirmed. Gold miners have massively underperformed gold itself. If the technical case holds, the miner leverage on a gold rally could be 2-4x the metal move.
items.tweetA: Tavi Costa this morning: 'There is nothing more technically bullish than a 40-year resistance level turning into major support. That is exactly where gold miners sit today.' I keep coming back to this chart. $GDX $GDXJ
items.tweetB: Gold miners at 40-year resistance flipped to support. Central banks buying 17 tonnes in April. AI demand driving real asset scarcity. Three separate reasons to look at gold miners — technical, structural demand, and macro. $GDX

charts.desc: Multiple chart sequence on gold bottom process — full series behind paywall

items.title: Broadcom disappointment → biggest Nasdaq/Dow gap in >1 year, but 360 S&P stocks STILL finished higher
items.idea: Broadcom delivered 'very good' not 'extraordinary' earnings. Semiconductor shares sold off; Nasdaq had its largest underperformance relative to the Dow in more than a year. Yet 360 companies within the S&P 500 finished higher. Healthcare had its strongest session in >1 year. Financials surged. Small caps experienced a powerful short squeeze. Capital rotated; it did not exit.
items.angle: If breadth is genuinely expanding and cyclical rotation is real, this is the setup for the 'everything rally' breaking the AI-concentration trade. Cyclical-minus-defensive positioning is at only the 17th percentile — enormous room to rotate in.
items.tweetA: Broadcom delivered 'very good' instead of 'extraordinary.' That caused the biggest Nasdaq/Dow gap in over a year. Meanwhile, 360 S&P stocks finished higher. Healthcare best session in >1yr. Small caps squeezed. The AI generals stumbled; the broader army kept marching.
items.tweetB: After months of AI semi concentration, Thursday was the first real rotation test: will capital rotate or leave? Answer: 360 S&P companies higher, healthcare surging, small-caps squeezing. Cyclical/defensive spread at only the 17th percentile. There is room left in this rotation.

items.source: Dark Side of the Boom / Challenger data
items.title: Challenger data: ~40% of all announced corporate layoffs are now directly AI-driven restructuring
items.idea: Challenger data shows nearly 40% of announced job cuts are linked directly to AI-driven restructuring. Companies are using AI not just to improve productivity but to replace existing roles. Weekly jobless claims moved higher simultaneously. The gap between survey data (optimistic) and hard labor market reality (softening) has widened to levels not seen in years.
items.angle: The AI bull market is simultaneously creating conditions for its own demand headwinds. Consumer spending is resilient — until AI-driven unemployment reaches the consumer balance sheet.
items.tweetA: Challenger data: 40% of announced corporate layoffs are now AI-driven restructuring. The stock market is celebrating AI productivity gains. The labor market is absorbing the displacement. Both are true simultaneously. The question is when the consumer balance sheet starts feeling it.
items.tweetB: Weekly jobless claims rising. 40% of corporate layoffs directly AI-attributed. Markets treat this as bullish because AI is doing the cutting. The day markets reframe AI layoffs as demand destruction will be violent. We're not there yet — but watch the claims data.

items.title: Dealer equity-linked financing has tripled in 2 years — the AI earnings boom is also a leveraged boom
items.idea: Dealer exposure to equity-linked financing (total return swaps, bank balance sheet leverage) has roughly tripled over the past two years. Options call-buying creates gamma squeeze mechanics: rising prices force dealers to buy more shares, which pushes prices higher, creating more buying. The core earnings story is genuine. Leverage is the accelerant layered on top.
items.angle: Three times the dealer leverage in 2 years is the classic setup for a violent corrective episode that resolves back into the bull market. The earnings story survives; the leverage does not.
items.tweetA: Dealer equity financing has tripled in 2 years. The AI boom is real — but it's increasingly a borrowed boom. The earnings story is genuine. The leverage layered on top is the accelerant. Both can coexist — until the feedback loop reverses.
items.tweetB: Total return swaps, dealer financing, call option gamma loops — the financial plumbing behind this rally has 3x'd in two years. The core AI thesis is solid. What concerns me isn't the thesis — it's the borrowed money chasing it. History says this ends in a flush, not a collapse.

items.title: OpenAI coders are 2-3x more productive with AI — and now overloading their own internal systems
items.idea: OpenAI's own engineers using Codex/AI coding tools have seen productivity jump 2-3x. Instead of pushing updates once or twice a week, they're pushing multiple times per week, causing cascading system failures: overloaded internal infrastructure, interrupted external customers. Same pattern at Microsoft/GitHub where AI agent traffic surges have caused platform outages. Parekh: every company in the world will experience this within 2-3 years.
items.angle: The deployment bottleneck is the next AI infrastructure problem nobody is pricing. This is a massive tailwind for deployment infrastructure, testing, and DevOps tooling.
items.tweetA: OpenAI's coders got 2-3x more productive with AI tools. Then their systems started failing. You can 3x your code output — but your deployment infrastructure was built for 1x. Every enterprise in the world will learn this lesson within 2-3 years.
items.tweetB: GitHub is having outages because AI agents are flooding the platform. OpenAI coders are deploying updates multiple times per week instead of once. Apps everywhere are getting slower. AI productivity has a deployment infrastructure ceiling — and we're hitting it.

items.title: AI Forward Deployed Engineers (FDEs) flooding enterprises — OpenAI + Anthropic creating multi-billion dollar consulting/PE partnerships
items.idea: FDEs — popularized by Palantir for defense — are now the hottest role in enterprise AI. OpenAI and Anthropic have created multi-billion dollar partnerships with consulting firms and PE to deploy FDEs inside enterprise customers. FDEs are paid better than other engineers at the same companies. Parekh: this is the AI-branded version of a multi-trillion dollar global IT services industry. India's IT sector has an underappreciated role here.
items.angle: The FDE wave is the consulting/professional services revenue stream for the AI era. Accenture, Deloitte, and India IT services firms are the quiet beneficiaries of AI enterprise rollout.
items.tweetA: The hottest job in enterprise AI is the one nobody tweets about: Forward Deployed Engineers. OpenAI + Anthropic have created multi-billion dollar consulting deals to put FDEs inside enterprise customers. Palantir pioneered this. Now every AI lab is copying it.
items.tweetB: AI FDEs are IT services consultants with a new name. Parekh's contrarian take: India's IT sector (Infosys, TCS, Wipro) has a ROLE here, not a death sentence. The AI-kills-India thesis may be exactly backwards. $INFY

items.title: Big tech stock-compensation taxes approaching $200B across industry — rivals annual AI capex
items.idea: Google's $80B+ equity raise includes $30-40B headed to stock-based-compensation taxes. Across all big tech, the annual SBC tax bill approaches $200B — comparable to what each company spends annually on AI data center capex. In a bull market, SBC tax obligations become a massive, hidden driver of equity issuance and capital flows.
items.angle: A circular capital flow invisible in most AI capex analysis: tech companies issue equity → to fund SBC taxes → employees sell → into the market → which keeps valuations elevated → which increases SBC grants.
items.tweetA: Google raised $80B+. $30-40B went to stock-compensation taxes. Across all big tech, the SBC tax bill approaches $200B — about equal to annual AI capex. You're not just funding data centers. You're funding employee tax bills.
items.tweetB: Buried in Alphabet's fine print: SBC taxes rival AI infrastructure spend. Across big tech, $200B flowing to SBC taxes annually. In a bull market this is invisible. In a bear market this becomes pure dilution. Watch the non-GAAP adjustments very carefully.

items.title: Google Gemma 4 12B: full multimodal AI (audio + video) running locally on a standard 16GB laptop
items.idea: Google's open-source Gemma 4 12B model analyzes audio AND video and runs entirely on a standard 16GB enterprise laptop. Nvidia's DGX Spark chip (announced with Microsoft) is the hardware complement. AI inference is moving from cloud-billed toward local, zero-marginal-cost compute on hundreds of millions of existing devices. If this scales, it directly dents Azure, AWS, and GCP AI revenue models.
items.angle: The commoditization of AI inference is accelerating. When 16GB laptops run multimodal models locally, the pricing power of cloud AI inference providers comes under structural pressure.
items.tweetA: Google's Gemma 4 12B: multimodal AI — audio + video — running locally on a standard 16GB enterprise laptop. No cloud, no token billing, no latency. We're about to move from per-token pricing to meterless local compute. Watch Azure AI revenue.
items.tweetB: Cloud AI is a-la-carte: you pay per token, per call, per second. Local AI is meterless. Google just shipped a 12B multimodal model that runs on a laptop. When 100M enterprise devices run local AI inference, the cloud toll business changes.

items.title: Venice ($VVV) hits ATH as BTC drops 3% — private AI inference routing as the core crypto AI thesis
items.idea: Venice ($VVV) made a new ATH while BTC dropped ~3%. Venice operates as a router — not a model builder. It aggregates AI models from OpenAI, Anthropic, and others and serves them without surveillance or content restrictions. Two-front strategy: win privacy-sensitive consumers first, then sell private inference infrastructure to AI agents. 'There's a lot of edge in being a router.'
items.angle: If AI privacy regulation tightens globally, Venice has structural first-mover advantage. Agent-to-agent inference is the next monetization layer — uncensored, private, composable.
items.tweetA: Venice ($VVV) hit an ATH yesterday while BTC fell 3%. The thesis: don't compete with OpenAI on models — aggregate them and serve them without surveillance. Then sell private inference to AI agents. In a world where privacy is increasingly valuable, the router owns the relationship.
items.tweetB: Everyone is building models. Venice is building the private inference layer between users/agents and all the models. Win privacy-focused consumers first. Monetize agent inference second. $VVV making ATHs while the market drops is a signal worth watching.

items.title: Coinbase launches SpaceX pre-IPO perpetual contract ahead of June 12 Nasdaq debut
items.idea: Coinbase launched a SpaceX pre-IPO perpetual futures contract for eligible non-US users, ahead of SpaceX's expected June 12 Nasdaq debut. The contract converts to a regular SpaceX perp after the IPO. The traditional IPO narrative is now bleeding into crypto derivatives markets — price discovery on the world's largest IPO is available 24/7 on crypto rails before the Nasdaq opens.
items.angle: By June 12, there will be weeks of futures sentiment data from crypto markets informing (or front-running) the Nasdaq open. The lines between TradFi IPO price discovery and DeFi derivatives are dissolving.
items.tweetA: Coinbase just launched a SpaceX pre-IPO perpetual contract. SpaceX lists June 12 on Nasdaq. You can trade SpaceX price discovery 24/7 on crypto rails right now, before the traditional market even opens. The gap between TradFi and DeFi price formation is closing fast.
items.tweetB: Pre-IPO SpaceX perps on Coinbase. By June 12, there will be weeks of futures data showing where institutional crypto money thinks SpaceX should trade. Traditional IPO pricing will have to compete with that signal. New market structure, happening right now.

items.title: BitMine: $300M preferred stock at 9.50% weekly dividend — funded by Ethereum staking yield
items.idea: BitMine announced a $300M preferred stock offering with a fixed 9.50% weekly dividend rate, using Ethereum staking yield to cover recurring payments — explicitly borrowing from Strategy's Bitcoin treasury capital-raising playbook. ETH staking yield is currently ~3.5%. The gap between the 9.50% preferred dividend and 3.5% staking yield implies ETH price appreciation or additional leverage closes the difference.
items.angle: This is financial innovation or financial engineering risk depending on ETH price. The Strategy playbook works because BTC is a store of value with institutional demand. Whether ETH staking yield can reliably fund a 9.50% preferred is the $300M question.
items.tweetA: BitMine: $300M preferred stock at 9.50% weekly dividend, funded by ETH staking yield. ETH staking yields ~3.5%. The preferred pays 9.50%. Either ETH price appreciation closes the 600bp gap or this is Strategy's playbook with significantly more embedded risk.
items.tweetB: A company is now using Ethereum staking yield as collateral for preferred dividends. Strategy made BTC treasury work because BTC doesn't yield — it just appreciates. ETH staking + preferred structure is more complex. Worth watching the mechanics here.

items.title: OpenAI at $25B ARR; Anthropic confidentially filed for IPO at $965B valuation; SpaceX is the opening act
items.idea: ARK reports: OpenAI crossed $25B annualized revenue as of May 2026 (up from ~$3-4B in 2024 — ~8x in roughly one year). Anthropic surpassed a $965B valuation and 'just confidentially filed for an IPO.' Databricks is preparing its own listing. ARK Venture Fund holds positions in six companies with active or near-term IPO timelines.
items.angle: OpenAI going from $3B to $25B ARR in ~12 months is the fastest revenue growth trajectory of any company in history. McDonald's does $25B annually. The question for the IPO: can they sustain even 50% growth from this base?
items.tweetA: OpenAI: $25B ARR as of May 2026. Anthropic confidentially filed for IPO at $965B valuation. Databricks next. SpaceX is the opening act. Three of the most consequential technology companies in history are hitting public markets simultaneously. This doesn't happen twice.
items.tweetB: OpenAI went from ~$3B to $25B ARR in roughly one year. McDonald's does $25B. We are watching a company grow faster than any enterprise in the history of organized commerce, and the market is debating whether AI is overhyped. Anthropic at $965B is filing now.

items.title: Bespoke analyzed 3 largest IPOs per year since 1998 — SpaceX, Anthropic, OpenAI hit the same window
items.idea: Bespoke assembled returns for the three largest US IPOs in each year since 1998, spanning all market regimes. Three massive offerings likely to hit this year: SpaceX (June 12 target), Anthropic (filed draft S-1, targeting early fall), and OpenAI (likely close behind). The research tests whether mega-IPOs systematically underperform. Historical comparisons: Visa underperformed initially then recovered; Saudi Aramco was a multi-year disappointment.
items.angle: The right historical parallel matters enormously. Visa and Facebook disappointed early buyers before becoming defining long-term holds. Aramco never recovered. Which frame applies to AI-era mega-IPOs depends on whether the revenue trajectories hold.
items.tweetA: Bespoke analyzed every top-3 IPO going back to 1998. The dataset they built is the most relevant historical lens for SpaceX (June 12), Anthropic (fall), and OpenAI. Historical precedent for mega-IPOs: mixed. Path-dependency of when you buy vs. when lockups expire matters enormously.
items.tweetB: Three of the largest IPOs in history — SpaceX, Anthropic, OpenAI — are likely to list within months of each other. Bespoke's 1998-to-now mega-IPO dataset is the right frame. The critical variable isn't the opening day return — it's the 12-month post-lockup performance.

charts.desc: Summary returns for top-3 annual IPOs since 1998 — behind paywall
